Name: Apolipoprotein A1 (ApoA1)
Description: ApoA1 is a protein carrier for HDL-C. It is the most abundant protein associated with HDL-C and serves as a cardiovascular disease risk indicator. Both HDL cholesterol and ApoA1 are anti-atherogenic — the higher the values, the lower the cardiovascular risk.
What It Measures: Protein carrier for HDL cholesterol; most informative biomarker for 'good' cholesterol and cardiovascular disease risk.
Optimal Ranges: Males: Normal 110–180 mg/dL; Optimal ≥115 mg/dL; At Risk <115 mg/dL. Females: Normal 110–205 mg/dL; Optimal ≥125 mg/dL; At Risk <125 mg/dL.
High Levels Mean: May be caused by genetic disorders, certain medications, or recent weight loss.
Low Levels Mean: May indicate diabetes, coronary artery disease, Tangier disease (levels <25 mg/dL), liver disorders, smoking, and poor lifestyle choices.
Health Implications: ApoA1 works to prevent plaque buildup in arteries through anti-atherogenic effects. Higher values correlate with lower cardiovascular risk.
How to Improve: Limit added sugar and sugar-sweetened beverages. Consume fermented dairy products. Avoid smoking. Increase physical activity. Achieve healthy weight.
References: University of Rochester Medical Center; PubMed — Apolipoprotein A-I and cholesterol efflux; Quest Diagnostics

Name: Apolipoprotein B (ApoB)
Description: An atherogenic particle that transports 'bad' cholesterol. Each particle of VLDL, IDL, LDL, or Lp(a) contains a single molecule of ApoB. Because there is only one ApoB per LDL particle, this measurement gives the most accurate estimate of the quantity of non-HDL cholesterol.
What It Measures: Measures additional types of 'bad cholesterol' beyond LDL-C alone. Better predictor of cardiovascular events than LDL-C in both men and women.
Optimal Ranges: General: Low <50 mg/dL; Optimal 50–65 mg/dL; Normal 65–100 mg/dL; High 100–120 mg/dL; High risk >120 mg/dL. SiPhox: Optimal ≤70; Good 70.1–89.9; Fair 90–104.9; Pay Attention 105–129.9; Alert ≥130 mg/dL.
High Levels Mean: May indicate pregnancy, high cholesterol, or kidney issues. Strongly associated with increased cardiovascular events and shortened lifespan.
Low Levels Mean: May indicate sepsis, liver disease, malabsorption, or high estrogen levels.
Health Implications: Superior predictor of cardiovascular risk compared to LDL-C alone. Elevated levels strongly associated with increased cardiovascular events.
How to Improve: Exercise ≥30 min daily. Maintain healthy weight. Avoid tobacco. Limit saturated/trans fats. Increase fruits, vegetables, omega-3 foods. Follow Mediterranean diet. Consult physician re: cholesterol-lowering drugs.
References: Cleveland Clinic; NIH/PubMed; MedlinePlus; The Lancet; Mayo Clinic Labs; AHA; Peter Attia MD

Name: Cortisol
Description: A hormone synthesized and secreted by the adrenal glands that plays a major role in regulating stress, metabolism, and immunity. Regulates how the body uses carbohydrates, fats, and proteins. Follows a diurnal rhythm affected by sleep patterns.
What It Measures: Cortisol levels in blood serum or saliva. A 3-point saliva test collects samples at morning, afternoon, and night to detect abnormalities in cortisol rhythm.
Optimal Ranges: Blood Serum — Morning (6–8 AM): 10–15 ug/dL; Evening (~4 PM): 4–10 ug/dL. Below 3 ug/dL may indicate adrenal insufficiency. Consistently above normal may indicate Cushing's disease.
High Levels Mean: Indicator of acute, chronic, or traumatic stress. Prolonged high levels (Cushing's disease) can lead to weight gain, fat redistribution, poor sleep, lowered immune system, and increased inflammation.
Low Levels Mean: May indicate poor adrenal function or Addison's disease.
Health Implications: Regulates carbohydrate, fat, and protein usage. Chronic elevation keeps blood sugar elevated and causes fat storage. Extended elevation leads to weight gain, poor sleep, lowered immunity, increased inflammation.
How to Improve: High: Ashwagandha, rhodiola, lemon balm, chamomile; dark chocolate, garlic, fermented foods, vitamin C; 8 hrs sleep; yoga, tai chi, meditation, time in nature. Low: High-intensity exercise, caffeine.
References: Cleveland Clinic; Huberman Lab; Multiple PubMed/NIH studies

Name: Estradiol (E2)
Description: A type of estrogen hormone naturally produced by both males and females. In females, regulates the menstrual cycle, plays a role in fertility and ovulation. In males, important for modulating libido, erectile function, and sperm synthesis. Also affects bone health and brain function.
What It Measures: Reproductive health status and overall hormonal balance.
Optimal Ranges: Out of range (low): <10 pg/mL; In range: 10–47 pg/mL; Out of range (high): >47 pg/mL. Ranges vary by personal context. Menstruating females: test on day 3 of cycle.
High Levels Mean: Females: pregnancy, ovulation, PCOS, ovarian tumors, liver disease. Males: erectile dysfunction, muscle loss, fatigue, mood changes, abdominal obesity, gynecomastia, cardiovascular disease, insulin resistance.
Low Levels Mean: Females: menopause, premature ovarian failure, pituitary/adrenal dysfunction; strongly linked to osteoporosis. Males: decreased libido and reduced sexual activity.
Health Implications: Operates beyond reproductive system including bone health and brain function. Deficiency in females strongly linked to osteoporosis. In males, high levels associated with cardiovascular disease and insulin resistance.
How to Improve: Increase: phytoestrogen-rich foods, boron, B vitamins, vitamin D, E, black cohosh, DHEA, red clover. Decrease: reduce body fat, stress management, limit alcohol, exercise, cruciferous vegetables.
References: Mount Sinai; NIH; Straight Healthcare

Name: Fasting Insulin
Description: Insulin is a hormone secreted primarily by the pancreas responsible for regulating blood glucose levels. Fasting insulin levels indicate how much insulin the body produces while fasted (no food/beverages besides water for 8 hours). Follows a natural circadian rhythm.
What It Measures: The amount of insulin the body produces while fasted; indicator of the body's reaction to diet and lifestyle.
Optimal Ranges: Low: <1.9 uIU/mL; Optimal: 1.9–8 uIU/mL; High: 8–23 uIU/mL; Very High: >23 uIU/mL. Requires 8-hour fast.
High Levels Mean: Signals insulin resistance — body fails to respond to insulin by taking up glucose. Leading indicator of pre-diabetes and metabolic syndrome. Symptoms: increased thirst, frequent urination, blurred vision, slow-healing wounds.
Low Levels Mean: Not specifically addressed beyond classification as 'Low' (<1.9 uIU/mL).
Health Implications: High insulin may precede type 2 diabetes. ~1 in 10 Americans have diabetes, many undiagnosed. Hormonal disorders and certain medications can also cause insulin resistance.
How to Improve: Avoid processed high-carb foods and saturated fats. Choose low glycemic index foods (beans, legumes, non-starchy veggies, berries). Exercise and lose weight. Stick to consistent eating schedules.
References: Cleveland Clinic; CDC; UVA Health; NCBI/PMC; Health Matters; NIDDK; ADA

Name: Ferritin
Description: The soluble, storage form of iron that provides an indirect measurement of the body's iron stores. An indirect indicator of physiological health related to energy production and oxygen transport.
What It Measures: Iron storage levels in the body.
Optimal Ranges: SiPhox optimal: 50–150 ng/mL (both sexes). Iron deficiency: <30 ng/mL. Iron deficiency anemia: <10 ng/mL. Iron overload: >150 ng/mL. Abnormal: >200 ng/mL females, >300 ng/mL males.
High Levels Mean: Iron overload. Can be elevated due to obesity and fatty liver. Associated conditions: inflammation, tissue destruction, liver disease, malignancies, hyperthyroidism, hemochromatosis, infection, cancer.
Low Levels Mean: Depletion of iron stores, iron-deficiency erythropoiesis, or iron deficiency anemia. Complications: fatigue, headaches, restless legs, heart problems, pregnancy complications, weakened immunity.
Health Implications: Essential for energy production and oxygen transport. Low ferritin associated with fatigue (especially <50 ng/mL), weakened immunity, cardiovascular complications. High ferritin can signal liver disease, cancer, hemochromatosis.
How to Improve: Low: iron-rich foods (cereals, oysters, lentils), pair with vitamin C, cook with cast iron, avoid phytates/polyphenols/calcium with meals. High: decrease iron-rich foods, limit alcohol, donate blood.
References: NIH; NHLBI; ASH Publications; Cleveland Clinic; Multiple NCBI/PMC studies

Name: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH)
Description: A hormone synthesized mostly by the pituitary gland involved in sexual development and reproduction. In females, stimulates estradiol production and regulates the menstrual cycle. In males, works with testosterone to maintain normal sperm count and function.
What It Measures: FSH levels indicating reproductive and sexual development status.
Optimal Ranges: Menstruating Females — Follicular: 2–9 mIU/mL; Mid-cycle peak: 4–22 mIU/mL; Luteal: 2–9 mIU/mL. Postmenopausal: >30 mIU/mL. Males: 1–7 mIU/mL. Test on day 3 of menstrual cycle.
High Levels Mean: Females: primary ovarian insufficiency, PCOS, perimenopause, ovarian tumor, Turner syndrome. Males: testicular damage, Klinefelter syndrome.
Low Levels Mean: Females: pituitary/hypothalamus disorder, insufficient egg production, being severely underweight. Males: pituitary/hypothalamus disorders.
Health Implications: Critical for reproductive function in both sexes. Abnormal levels can indicate ovarian/testicular failure to pituitary/hypothalamic disorders. Key marker for menopause and fertility assessment.
How to Improve: Increase: exercise (HIIT), oyster polypeptides, melatonin. Decrease: weight loss, probiotics, royal jelly, omega-3, vitamin D, zinc.
References: MedlinePlus; Straight Healthcare; ABIM; Multiple PubMed studies

Name: HDL-C
Description: Often referred to as 'good' cholesterol because it helps remove other forms of cholesterol from the bloodstream. HDL-C carries cholesterol away from arteries back to the liver for processing and removal.
What It Measures: Cardiovascular health risk and cholesterol metabolism. The triglyceride-to-HDL-C ratio is one particularly valuable marker for metabolic syndrome risk.
Optimal Ranges: General: Abnormally low <23 mg/dL; Worrisome 23–60; Excellent 60–92; Elevated 92–100; Abnormally high >100 mg/dL. SiPhox: Optimal ≥80; Good 60–79.99; Fair 40–59.9; Pay Attention <40 mg/dL.
High Levels Mean: Abnormally high HDL-C (>100 mg/dL) may paradoxically increase heart disease risk despite historical assumptions of universal protection.
Low Levels Mean: Abnormally low (<23 mg/dL): may indicate severe hypertriglyceridemia. Worrisome (23–60): may indicate diabetes or coronary heart disease.
Health Implications: High levels associated with lower cardiovascular disease risk; low levels with increased risk. Not as strong an indicator as ApoB or LDL-C.
How to Improve: Eat fruits, vegetables, omega-3 foods, soluble fiber. Follow Mediterranean or DASH diet. Avoid animal fats, saturated fats, salt. 150 min moderate or 75 min high-intensity exercise/week. Avoid smoking. Wine in moderation.
References: CDC/NCHS; NCBI/NIH; Harvard Health; Cleveland Clinic; Mayo Clinic; UMass Chan

Name: High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP)
Description: An acute inflammatory protein produced by the liver in response to infection or inflammation. Quantifies CRP at extremely small concentrations to identify low but persistent inflammation.
What It Measures: General inflammation levels; marker for heart disease and stroke risk. Lower values indicate better health status.
Optimal Ranges: AHA: Healthy <1.0 mg/L; Slightly High 1.0–1.5; Moderate Risk 1.0–10; High Risk >10; Critical >50 mg/L. SiPhox: Optimal ≤0.75; Good 0.76–1.4; Fair 1.5–1.99; Pay Attention ≥2.0 mg/L.
High Levels Mean: Minor elevation (up to 1.0): obesity, pregnancy, depression, sedentary lifestyle. Moderate (1.0–10): autoimmune disease, cardiovascular events. High risk (>10): infection or trauma.
Low Levels Mean: Lower levels indicate better cardiovascular health and lower inflammation status. Optimal levels associated with reduced cardiovascular disease risk.
Health Implications: Risk factors: male sex, older age, obesity, pregnancy, depression, sedentary lifestyle, smoking, genetics. Elevated levels correlate with increased cardiovascular disease and stroke risk.
How to Improve: Anti-inflammatory foods (tart cherry juice), Mediterranean/DASH diet. Exercise, quit smoking, oral hygiene, sauna 2–3x/week. Supplements: curcumin, magnesium, omega-3, vitamin D, zinc+vitamin A+magnesium.
References: NCBI; Kaiser Permanente; AHA; Cleveland Heart Lab; Mayo Clinic Labs; Multiple PubMed studies

Name: Homocysteine
Description: A naturally produced, sulfur-containing amino acid metabolized to create essential compounds such as methionine and cysteine.
What It Measures: Homocysteine levels in blood plasma during fasting state.
Optimal Ranges: AHA: Low <5 umol/L; Optimal 5–9; Normal 5–15; Moderate hyperhomocysteinemia 16–30; Intermediate 31–100; Severe >100 umol/L. SiPhox: Optimal <4; Good 4–5.9; Fair 6–9.9; Pay Attention 10–14.9; Alert >15 umol/L.
High Levels Mean: Increases cardiovascular risk via monocyte activation promoting inflammatory response. Associated with coronary artery disease, heart attacks, blood clots, strokes, neurological disorders, dementia.
Low Levels Mean: Not specifically addressed beyond classification as low (<5 umol/L).
Health Implications: Elevated levels increase cardiovascular risk by activating monocytes and triggering inflammatory responses. Causes: low B vitamins (B6, B12), low folate, aging, alcohol, smoking, thyroid/kidney disease, genetics.
How to Improve: Reduce stress, cut back on coffee, limit alcohol, quit smoking, resistance training. Supplement with B vitamins (B6, B12, folic acid).
References: Cleveland Clinic; AHA; University of Rochester; Michael Lustgarten PhD; JAMA Neurology; Multiple PubMed studies

Name: LDL-C
Description: Often referred to as 'bad' cholesterol because high levels can lead to cholesterol accumulation in arteries, contributing to atherosclerosis and increasing cardiovascular disease and stroke risk. Carries cholesterol from the liver to body cells.
What It Measures: Concentration of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol in blood; key marker of cardiovascular risk assessment.
Optimal Ranges: Johns Hopkins: Optimal 40–100 mg/dL; Elevated 100–130; Borderline High 130–160; High 160–190; Very High >190 mg/dL. No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Increased atherosclerosis risk, plaque formation in arteries, cardiovascular disease and stroke risk.
Low Levels Mean: Very low levels can have negative implications though specifics not detailed. VLDL, IDL, LDL, Lp(a) particles in moderation are healthy.
Health Implications: Remains valuable in cardiovascular risk assessment. Included in SiPhox Health Foundation panel.
How to Improve: Avoid saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol. Increase soluble fiber. Maintain healthy weight. Exercise regularly. Quit smoking. Limit alcohol. Whey protein supplementation can decrease LDL-C.
References: Cleveland Clinic; MedlinePlus; Mayo Clinic; CDC; Johns Hopkins Medicine; NCBI; Peter Attia MD

Name: Testosterone
Description: The primary male hormone responsible for sexual health and development. Proper levels essential for both males and females. An imbalance can negatively affect reproductive and overall health.
What It Measures: Two measurements: Total Testosterone (free + bound) and Free Testosterone (unbound, more bioactive).
Optimal Ranges: Males Total (ng/mL): Age 20–49: 3.0–10.8; Age 40–60: 3.0–8.9; Age 60+: 3.0–7.2. Males Free: ≥18: 0.047–0.244 ng/mL. Females Serum: 0.18–0.54 ng/mL. Low: <3.0 ng/mL (AUA).
High Levels Mean: Females: hyperandrogenism — PCOS, adrenal disorders, ovarian tumors, certain medications.
Low Levels Mean: Males: reduced sex drive, erectile dysfunction, low sperm count, emotional changes. Females: primary ovarian insufficiency, adrenal insufficiency, pituitary disorders.
Health Implications: Males: regulates libido, bone mass, fat distribution, muscle mass, RBC and sperm production. Females: maintains muscle mass, bone density, libido, reproductive health, mood. Test 7–10 AM for accuracy.
How to Improve: Fenugreek, ashwagandha, honey. Adequate zinc intake. Avoid chronic stress. Better sleep. Lower abdominal fat. Strength training and aerobic exercise. Saw palmetto or boron supplements.
References: NCBI; Cleveland Clinic; Cincinnati Children's Hospital; ABIM; Multiple PubMed/NCBI studies

Name: Testosterone:Cortisol Ratio
Description: Demonstrates an important relationship between two critical hormonal biomarkers. Testosterone functions as a major anabolic hormone while cortisol is linked to catabolism (breakdown of products).
What It Measures: Comparative balance between testosterone (anabolic/building) and cortisol (catabolic/breakdown) to assess hormonal health status.
Optimal Ranges: No specific numerical ranges provided. Test testosterone 7–10 AM and cortisol 6–8 AM for accuracy due to diurnal fluctuations.
High Levels Mean: Well-rested conditions, effective training, or peak performance periods. Enhanced tissue repair, muscle growth, athletic performance. Potential association with social aggression.
Low Levels Mean: High stress, overtraining, inadequate recovery, illness.
Health Implications: Generally linked to athletic performance. Athletes seek to increase this ratio for enhanced recovery and muscle development.
How to Improve: Focus on improving testosterone and cortisol levels individually. See separate biomarker pages for specific strategies.
References: Open Sports Sciences Journal; Gray's Medicine; MDPI; Oxford Academic; ScienceDirect; PubMed

Name: Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH)
Description: Produced by the pituitary gland following TRH secretion from the hypothalamus. Stimulates the thyroid to produce T4 and T3. T4 converts to active T3 primarily in liver, kidneys, brain. T3 inhibits TRH and TSH via negative feedback.
What It Measures: Health of the thyroid gland and endocrine function. Thyroid regulates metabolism, growth/development, body temperature, energy levels.
Optimal Ranges: UCLA Health: Very low 0–0.5; Low 0.5–1; Optimal 1–2; High 2–5.0; Very high >5 uIU/mL. SiPhox: Optimal 0.45–5.33; Pay Attention >5.33; Alert >10 uIU/mL.
High Levels Mean: Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid). Symptoms: lethargy, weight gain, cold sensitivity, thinning hair, constipation. May indicate Graves' or Hashimoto's disease.
Low Levels Mean: Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid). Symptoms: weight loss, rapid/irregular heartbeat, increased hunger, anxiousness.
Health Implications: TSH changes can signal early thyroid problems. Follows circadian rhythm — morning testing may better detect hypothyroidism.
How to Improve: Lower TSH: avoid excess iodine; myoinositol+selenium; zinc, vitamin A, magnesium; ashwagandha. Raise TSH: Mediterranean/vegetarian diet; vitamin D; bugleweed; lemon balm; CoQ10; vitamin E. Quit smoking, exercise, improve sleep.
References: Tulane University; MedlinePlus; UCLA Health; Cleveland Clinic; Mayo Clinic; NIH; Multiple peer-reviewed studies

Name: Total Cholesterol
Description: A type of lipid important for cell membranes and hormones, contributing to digestion and metabolism. Dietary sources account for only 20–30% of circulating levels; most is synthesized by the body.
What It Measures: Total amount of cholesterol in the bloodstream, processed in the liver.
Optimal Ranges: Very Low <125 mg/dL; Low 125–160; Optimal 160–200; At Risk 200–240; Dangerous >240 mg/dL. No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Excessive cholesterol can cause atherosclerosis (plaque buildup) potentially leading to cardiovascular events including heart disease or stroke (hyperlipidemia).
Low Levels Mean: Abnormally low (hypolipidemia) may indicate anemia, cancer, hepatitis C, malabsorption, hyperthyroidism, or undernutrition.
Health Implications: High: cardiovascular disease risk, atherosclerosis. Low: signal of underlying systemic conditions.
How to Improve: Mediterranean diet, plant-based diets, increase fruits/vegetables and soluble fiber, reduce saturated fat. Exercise, avoid smoking, maintain healthy weight, manage blood pressure and blood sugar, limit alcohol.
References: Better Health Victoria; Cleveland Clinic; Peter Attia MD; Merck Manuals; Nature; NCBI/PMC; AHA

Name: Total Cholesterol:HDL Ratio
Description: An important metric of cardiovascular health comparing bad and good cholesterol.
What It Measures: Ratio calculated by dividing total cholesterol by HDL-C level. Total cholesterol comprises LDL-C, HDL-C, and 20% of triglycerides.
Optimal Ranges: Very Good <3.5; Low Risk <4.5; Moderate Risk 4.5–6; High Risk >6. No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Greater risk of cardiovascular events. Even when HDL-C is individually elevated, higher LDL-C can still elevate overall risk.
Low Levels Mean: Lower ratios indicate reduced cardiovascular risk.
Health Implications: Predicts acute myocardial infarction, coronary heart disease, cardiovascular morbidity and mortality.
How to Improve: Refer to individual strategies for Total Cholesterol, HDL-C, LDL-C, and Triglycerides.
References: University of Rochester Medical Center; Cleveland Clinic; NCBI publications

Name: Triglycerides
Description: The primary storage form of energy in animals. Excess fat is processed and stored in the liver as triglycerides. Mobilized during fasting through lipolysis.
What It Measures: Levels of triglycerides in the bloodstream.
Optimal Ranges: Low 0–100 mg/dL; Optimal 70–100; Normal 100–150; Borderline High 150–199; High 200–499; Very High >500 mg/dL. Under 50 may indicate hypotriglyceridemia.
High Levels Mean: Increased atherosclerosis risk leading to carotid artery disease, heart attack, metabolic syndrome, stroke. 1 in 4 Americans have high triglycerides. Associated: diabetes, thyroid disease, medications, family history.
Low Levels Mean: Under 50 mg/dL may indicate hypotriglyceridemia.
Health Implications: Cardiovascular event risk, metabolic syndrome, obesity/type 2 diabetes association. Included in SiPhox base panel.
How to Improve: Limit sugar and refined foods. Eat more fiber. Avoid saturated fats. Maintain healthy weight. Exercise regularly. Manage blood pressure and diabetes. Quit smoking. Abstain from alcohol. Adequate sleep. Reduce stress.
References: Cleveland Clinic; ABIM; MedlinePlus; NCBI/PubMed; Peter Attia MD; AHA; The Lancet

Name: Hemoglobin A1C (HbA1c)
Description: Correlates to average blood sugar levels over the past 2–3 months by measuring the percentage of hemoglobin proteins coated with sugar.
What It Measures: Percentage of hemoglobin proteins with sugar coating; provides long-term snapshot of blood glucose concentration.
Optimal Ranges: General: Optimal 4–5%; Normal 4–5.6%; Pre-diabetes 5.7–6.4%; Diabetes >6.5%; Critical >9%. SiPhox: Optimal ≤5.2%; Good 5.3–5.5%; Fair 5.6–5.9%; Pay Attention 6.0–6.5%; Alert ≥6.5%.
High Levels Mean: Levels consistently above 5.6% may warrant physician visit. Elevated A1C indicates increased diabetic risk.
Low Levels Mean: Not explicitly detailed; levels within optimal range (4–5%) indicate healthy blood sugar management.
Health Implications: Primary screening tool for diabetic risk. Factors affecting results: kidney failure, liver disease, anemia, uncommon hemoglobin types, certain medications, blood loss/transfusions, pregnancy.
How to Improve: Increase fiber (veggies, whole grains, fruit). Weight loss. Regular exercise (aerobic and strength training). Smoking cessation.
References: CDC; Mayo Clinic; Johns Hopkins Medicine; MedlinePlus; ABIM; Nursing Center

Name: Vitamin D
Description: A fat-soluble vitamin synthesized through UV light exposure or obtained from food and supplements. Plays crucial roles in calcium absorption, bone mineralization, inflammation reduction, and neuromuscular and metabolic functions.
What It Measures: Vitamin D serum concentration (25-hydroxyvitamin D). Indicates adequacy for bone health, immune function, and metabolic regulation.
Optimal Ranges: Deficiency <25 ng/mL; Insufficient 25–40; Sufficient 40–70; High 70–100; Critical >100 ng/mL. No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Excessive vitamin D can cause nausea, dehydration, kidney issues; severe cases: anorexia, irregular heartbeat, hardening of blood vessels and tissues.
Low Levels Mean: Decreased bone health, muscular atrophy, rickets, osteomalacia. Linked to elevated inflammatory markers and increased Metabolic Syndrome risk. At-risk: vegans, insufficient sun, obesity, gastric bypass, IBD/Crohn's.
Health Implications: Essential for calcium absorption, bone mineralization, immune function, inflammation regulation, neuromuscular function, metabolic health.
How to Improve: Dietary: fatty fish, fortified OJ/cereal/milk. Lifestyle: 15–20 min outdoors 10am–3pm. Supplementation: 600 IU daily recommended.
References: National Library of Medicine; NIH; Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health

Name: Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT)
Description: An enzyme primarily concentrated in liver cells. Also present in kidneys, heart, muscles, pancreas. Its high liver concentration makes it a significant marker for hepatic health. More specific for liver damage than AST.
What It Measures: Liver health and hepatic cell integrity. When liver cells sustain damage, ALT releases into the bloodstream at elevated levels.
Optimal Ranges: Standard: Adults ~11–52 U/L (women typically lower). SiPhox: Optimal ≤25; Good 25.1–34.9; Fair 35–44.9; Pay Attention ≥45; Alert >80 U/L.
High Levels Mean: Liver inflammation or damage. Chronic elevation warrants investigation. Slight elevations may be normal for some individuals.
Low Levels Mean: Not addressed.
Health Implications: Early diagnosis of liver diseases, monitoring disease progression or treatment effectiveness. Important alongside AST and other liver enzymes.
How to Improve: Not specifically addressed on this page.
References: Giannini (2005); Kalas et al. (2021); Cleveland Clinic; Medscape

Name: Creatinine
Description: A waste product generated from protein digestion and normal muscle wear and tear. Produced from creatine (essential for muscle energy). Kidneys filter creatinine for urinary excretion.
What It Measures: Kidney function and renal health. Blood levels indicate whether kidneys are functioning properly.
Optimal Ranges: Adult Males: 0.70–1.30 mg/dL; Adult Females: 0.50–1.10 mg/dL. Higher muscle mass correlates with higher creatinine.
High Levels Mean: Impaired kidney function or kidney disease; kidneys not filtering effectively.
Low Levels Mean: Not specified.
Health Implications: Marker of chronic kidney disease (CKD), guides treatment decisions including dialysis necessity, indicates disease progression.
How to Improve: Limit meat intake; increase high-fiber foods. Stay hydrated, quit tobacco, limit sodium, avoid NSAIDs.
References: ABIM; American Kidney Fund; UCLA Health; National Kidney Organization

Name: eGFR (Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate)
Description: Assesses how well kidneys function by estimating blood filtered by glomeruli each minute. Calculated based on creatinine, age, body size, and gender. Non-invasive kidney health monitor.
What It Measures: How efficiently kidneys filter waste from blood.
Optimal Ranges: General (UK Kidney Association): Healthy ~60–120 mL/min/1.73 m². SiPhox: Optimal ≥100; Good 90–99.9; Fair 80.1–89.9; Pay Attention ≤80; Alert <60 mL/min/1.73 m². Must be interpreted with other clinical findings.
High Levels Mean: Normal to optimal kidney function with effective filtration capacity (≥90).
Low Levels Mean: Reduced kidney function. Below 60 warrants immediate physician consultation. Impaired kidneys can cause heart disease, stroke, high blood pressure, weak bones, nerve damage, low RBC count.
Health Implications: Essential for diagnosing kidney conditions, assessing damage severity, guiding treatment. Important for those with diabetes, hypertension, or heart disease. 1 in 3 American adults at risk for kidney disease.
How to Improve: Reduce salt; moderate protein (low-protein diet can preserve renal function in CKD). Weight management, exercise, quit smoking, limit alcohol, avoid NSAIDs, annual flu shots, exercise therapy.
References: National Kidney Foundation; American Kidney Fund; NIDDK; UK Kidney Association; AJKD; CDC; NCBI studies

Name: Fasting Glucose
Description: The concentration of glucose in blood after an overnight fast. Provides insight into the body's ability to maintain and regulate blood sugar levels within a narrow range during fasting.
What It Measures: The body's capability to maintain glucose regulation during fasting and metabolic health status. Requires minimum 8-hour overnight fast.
Optimal Ranges: Normal <100 mg/dL; Prediabetes (Impaired Fasting Glucose) 100–125; Diabetes ≥126 mg/dL (on two separate tests). No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Hyperglycemia — impaired glucose metabolism, increased diabetes and cardiovascular risk. Symptoms: increased thirst, frequent urination, fatigue, blurred vision, slow wound healing.
Low Levels Mean: Hypoglycemia. Symptoms: hunger, weakness, blurred vision, difficulty concentrating, confusion, dizziness, nausea.
Health Implications: Critical for diagnosing prediabetes, diabetes mellitus, insulin resistance. ~38.4 million Americans have diabetes; ~8.7 million undiagnosed.
How to Improve: Low-carbohydrate diets, adequate sleep, regular physical activity, healthy weight, stress management. Prescribed medications or insulin therapy if needed. CGM systems for real-time tracking.
References: National Library of Medicine; Mayo Clinic; CDC; Cleveland Clinic; NIDDK

Name: HOMA-IR
Description: Estimates insulin resistance — a condition where cells become less responsive to insulin. Reflects beta cell function and insulin sensitivity. Formula: (Fasting Insulin × Fasting Glucose) / 405.
What It Measures: Insulin resistance level.
Optimal Ranges: General: Insulin Sensitive <1.0; Borderline 1.5–2.9; Significantly Resistant >2.9. SiPhox: Optimal ≤1; Good 1.1–1.49; Fair 1.5–1.99; Pay Attention ≥2.
High Levels Mean: Greater insulin resistance, increasing risk for type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, dyslipidemia, hypertension, obesity.
Low Levels Mean: Good insulin sensitivity, low diabetes risk.
Health Implications: Crucial for maintaining and assessing metabolic health. Provides quantifiable measure of insulin resistance. Early detection enables timely interventions.
How to Improve: Mediterranean diets, high-protein diets, low-calorie diets. HIIT, moderate-intensity exercise, strength training, resistance training. Sufficient sleep (~8 hours).
References: BMC Endocrine Disorders; American Journal of Gastroenterology; Dr. Eric Berg; Studies on diet interventions

Name: Lipoprotein(a) — Lp(a)
Description: A lipoprotein that transports cholesterol in the bloodstream, similar to LDL-P. About 1 in 5 people worldwide have high Lp(a), including 63 million in the US.
What It Measures: Cholesterol transport particles in blood. Measured in nmol/L (preferred) or mg/dL.
Optimal Ranges: Standard: Optimal <14 mg/dL; Borderline 14–30; High risk 31–50; Highest risk >50 mg/dL. SiPhox: Optimal <50 nmol/L; Good 50–74; Fair 75–124; Pay Attention ≥125 nmol/L. EAS: Normal <30 mg/dL.
High Levels Mean: Increased cardiovascular risk via plaque buildup, increased clotting, inflammation causing plaque rupture. Higher risk for atherosclerosis, heart attack, stroke.
Low Levels Mean: Reduced cardiovascular risk. Not explicitly addressed.
Health Implications: Genetically determined, not significantly affected by lifestyle. Conditions that may alter: diabetes, CKD, post-menopause. African and South Asian descent face higher risk.
How to Improve: More low-calorie nutrient-dense foods; lean protein; monounsaturated fats. Aerobic exercise ≥30 min/day 5x/week; resistance training ≥2 days/week. Healthy weight, adequate sleep, tobacco cessation. Research remains inconclusive.
References: CDC; AHA; ACC; Cleveland Clinic; Johns Hopkins; Mayo Clinic; MedlinePlus; NLM; Multiple studies

Name: Albumin
Description: A protein manufactured by the liver and one of the most abundant proteins in blood. Serves critical functions including transporting hormones, vitamins, and enzymes.
What It Measures: Protein levels in blood, liver function, kidney function, nutritional status, hormone transport capacity (particularly testosterone).
Optimal Ranges: Normal range: 3.5–5.5 g/dL; Optimal concentration: 4.0 g/dL (UNC Kidney Center). No sex-specific or tiered categories.
High Levels Mean: Dehydration, excess protein intake, use of anabolic steroids or supplements.
Low Levels Mean: Malnutrition, burns/infections, liver dysfunction/disease, kidney disease (albuminuria), impaired nutrient absorption.
Health Implications: Especially significant to testosterone transport — accounts for 50–68% of total testosterone in males. Reduced albumin correlates with decreased biologically active free testosterone.
How to Improve: Low: balanced diet with protein, dairy, whole grains; limit sodium; reduce alcohol; consider vitamin D. High: increase water, exercise, quit smoking, limit alcohol, maintain healthy weight.
References: MedlinePlus; UNC Kidney Center; Cleveland Clinic; National Kidney Foundation; Doctor O'Donovan

Name: Anti-Mullerian Hormone (AMH)
Description: An important reproductive health biomarker produced by testes in males and ovaries in females. Most accurate predictor of ovarian reserve.
What It Measures: Ovarian reserve (egg supply in females), reproductive function, sperm and hormonal production (males).
Optimal Ranges: Age-specific 'Good' (Females): Age 25 >3.0; Age 30 >2.5; Age 35 >1.5; Age 40 >1.0; Age 45 >0.5 ng/mL. General: Average 1–3; Low <1; Severely low <0.5 ng/mL. Testable anytime during cycle.
High Levels Mean: Greater egg supply/ovarian reserve, better fertility prospects. Possible PCOS in some cases.
Low Levels Mean: Shortage of eggs, increased pregnancy difficulty, approaching menopause, diminished ovarian reserve. Low AMH may still conceive naturally — does not provide egg quality info.
Health Implications: Guides IVF planning, diagnoses irregular periods, tracks menopausal onset, aids PCOS/ovarian cancer diagnosis.
How to Improve: Dairy products (may slow AMH decline); decrease dietary fat; pro-fertility diet for overweight/obese; regular exercise; quit smoking; vitamin D supplementation.
References: MedlinePlus; Cleveland Clinic; NCBI studies; Shady Grove Fertility; Dr. Jolene Brighten

Name: Folate (Vitamin B9)
Description: The natural dietary form of vitamin B9. Folic acid is the synthetic form with better absorption. Both essential for multiple physiological functions.
What It Measures: Folate levels in blood serum, indicating adequacy for cellular and developmental health.
Optimal Ranges: Reference range: 2.7–17.0 ng/mL; Adequate level: >3 ng/mL. No tiered or sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Not discussed.
Low Levels Mean: Linked to Alzheimer's disease and depression. Can increase homocysteine levels and impair cell growth and function.
Health Implications: Critical for healthy cell growth, fetal development, reducing birth defect risk, brain function, homocysteine breakdown.
How to Improve: Dietary: spinach, peas, grains, liver. Adults: 400 mcg daily; pregnant: 600+ mcg (max 1000 mcg). Supplementation available.
References: Mayo Clinic; Mount Sinai; NIH; Harvard School of Public Health; Nucleus Medical Media

Name: Luteinizing Hormone (LH)
Description: A crucial hormone for reproductive health. Stimulates ovulation in females and testosterone production in males.
What It Measures: Triggers egg release and corpus luteum development in females. Stimulates Leydig cells for testosterone production in males.
Optimal Ranges: Male: Normal 2–9 mIU/mL. Female: Follicular 1.68–15; Midcycle peak 21.9–56.6; Luteal 0.61–16.3; Postmenopausal 14.2–52.3 mIU/mL. Test on day 3 of cycle.
High Levels Mean: Males: testicular damage or Klinefelter's syndrome. Females: PCOS or Turner syndrome.
Low Levels Mean: Males: pituitary or hypothalamic issues. Females: malnutrition, eating disorders, pituitary/hypothalamic disorders.
Health Implications: Males: testing for infertility, low libido, low sperm count. Females: determine infertility causes, predict ovulation, confirm menopause. Not to be used as birth control.
How to Improve: Vegetarian diet to lower LH; increase fiber; yoga; quit smoking; healthy weight loss; vitamin E supplementation.
References: MedlinePlus; ABIM; University of Rochester; Peer-reviewed studies

Name: Prolactin (PRL)
Description: A hormone synthesized primarily by the pituitary gland. In women, regulates milk production and menstrual cycle. In men, affects testosterone production, libido, erectile function, and fertility. Releases in response to stress and sleep.
What It Measures: Pituitary gland function and reproductive hormone balance.
Optimal Ranges: Men: <20 ng/mL; Nonpregnant Women: <25 ng/mL; Pregnant Women: 80–400 ng/mL. Optimal testing: 2–3 hours after waking, fasting.
High Levels Mean: Men: inhibits testosterone, decreased energy/muscle, anemia, ED, low sperm. Women: menstrual irregularities, vaginal dryness, galactorrhea. Causes: antipsychotics, opiates, infections, pituitary tumors.
Low Levels Mean: Men: negative metabolic, psychological, sexual characteristics. Women: impedes breast milk production, may increase cardiovascular/metabolic complications.
Health Implications: Important indicator of pituitary function, reproductive health, and metabolic status in both sexes.
How to Improve: Lower: vitamin C foods, limit gluten, limit alcohol, reduce stress, vitamin B6, E, zinc, ashwagandha. Raise (lactation): natural/pharmacologic galactagogues, fenugreek.
References: Mount Sinai; NIH/NCBI; UpToDate; Synlab; ScienceDirect; Springer Link

Name: Prostate-Specific Antigen (PSA)
Description: A protein produced by the prostate gland that plays a role in maintaining semen in liquid state, facilitating sperm movement.
What It Measures: Prostate health and potential disease. Screens for prostate problems, monitors cancer treatment, assesses recurrence risk.
Optimal Ranges: Age-stratified (Cleveland Clinic): 40–50: Normal 0–2.5 ng/mL; 50–60: Normal 2.5–3.5; 60–70: Normal 3.5–4.5; 70–80: Normal 4.5–5.5 ng/mL. Abnormal exceeds upper limit for age.
High Levels Mean: PSA 4–10: 25% likelihood of prostate cancer. PSA >10: >50% likelihood. May indicate enlarged prostate, inflammation, or cancer. Not all elevated PSA indicates cancer.
Low Levels Mean: No specific clinical implications mentioned.
Health Implications: Prostate cancer affects 1 in 8 men and is second leading cancer death in men. Used to monitor treatment efficacy.
How to Improve: Lycopene via red/pink fruits and vegetables; plant-based diets; coffee; green tea. Increase physical activity; quit smoking. Vitamin D supplementation.
References: American Cancer Society; Cleveland Clinic; Mayo Clinic; NCBI; PubMed

Name: Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin (SHBG)
Description: A protein made by the liver that carries and regulates sex hormones. When hormones bind to SHBG they become inactive — only free (unbound) hormones are biologically available.
What It Measures: Hormone bioavailability. Males test when SHBG is elevated (reducing free testosterone); females test when SHBG is low (increasing active testosterone).
Optimal Ranges: Males: 13.3–89.5 nmol/L (Mayo). Males age-specific: Under 50: 18.3–54.8; Over 50: 20.6–76.7 nmol/L. Females menstruating: 18.2–135.5; Menopausal: 16.8–125.2 nmol/L. SHBG increases with age in males, decreases in females.
High Levels Mean: Associated with pregnancy, weight loss, hepatitis, hemochromatosis, hyperthyroidism. In males: excess binds more testosterone, reducing bioavailable testosterone, causing low libido, ED, infertility.
Low Levels Mean: Associated with obesity, insulin resistance, metabolic disease, diabetes, PCOS, fatty liver. In females: abnormally high active testosterone causing infertility, acne, irregular menstruation, excessive hair growth.
Health Implications: Males with normal total T but low libido/ED benefit from SHBG testing. Females with infertility, acne, menstrual irregularities warrant assessment.
How to Improve: Lower elevated SHBG: increase protein, quit smoking, abstain from alcohol, boron supplementation. Raise low SHBG: limit saturated fat, increase fiber, olive oil, healthy BMI, exercise, vitamin D.
References: MedlinePlus; University of Rochester; Mayo Clinic Labs; Exeter Clinical Lab; Peer-reviewed studies

Name: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin)
Description: An essential nutrient the body cannot produce independently. Supports red blood cell production, DNA synthesis, and nervous system development/function. Obtained from animal products, nutritional yeast, or fortified foods.
What It Measures: Vitamin B12 serum levels indicating adequacy for blood cell production, nerve function, and DNA synthesis.
Optimal Ranges: Reference range: 180–914 pg/mL; Normal: 200–800 pg/mL; Suboptimal: <200 pg/mL. No sex-specific or tiered ranges.
High Levels Mean: Not addressed.
Low Levels Mean: Pernicious anemia, weakness/fatigue, nerve damage (tingling, numbness, balance problems), memory difficulties, dementia risk. Particularly concerning in older adults.
Health Implications: Critical for anemia prevention, nerve function, healthy aging, hormonal regulation, fertility, sperm health.
How to Improve: B12-rich foods: clams, beef liver, nutritional yeast. Adults: 2.4 mcg daily; pregnant: 2.6 mcg; breastfeeding: 2.8 mcg. Supplementation available.
References: NIH; ARUP Laboratory; Mount Sinai; Healthline

Name: Free T3 (Triiodothyronine)
Description: The active form of thyroid hormone circulating in blood that can enter tissues and exert biological effects. Unlike bound T3, free T3 is bioavailable. Produced when thyroid converts inactive T4, accounting for ~80% of total T3 synthesis.
What It Measures: Active thyroid hormone levels. Regulates metabolism, heart/digestive functions, muscle control, brain development, bone maintenance.
Optimal Ranges: ABIM: Low <1.9 pg/mL; Normal 1.9–4.4; Optimal 3.2–3.9; High >4.4 pg/mL. No sex-specific ranges. Follows circadian rhythm — peaks ~4am, nadir ~4pm. Morning testing recommended.
High Levels Mean: May suggest hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid). Symptoms: weight loss, rapid/irregular heartbeat, increased hunger, anxiousness.
Low Levels Mean: May suggest hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid). Symptoms: lethargy, weight gain, cold sensitivity, hair thinning, constipation.
Health Implications: Thyroid hormones vital to metabolism, heart/digestive functions, muscle control, brain development, bone maintenance.
How to Improve: High: reduce iodine; more cruciferous vegetables. Low: adequate calories and carbohydrates; manage inflammation; reduce stress; improve sleep quality and duration.
References: Cleveland Clinic; MedlinePlus; ABIM; Oxford University Press

Name: Free T4 (Thyroxine)
Description: One of two main thyroid hormones. T4 is inactive and converted to active T3 mainly in the liver, kidneys, brain. Unlike bound T4, free T4 can readily enter body tissues. Free T4 test provides more accurate information than total T4 test.
What It Measures: Thyroid gland health and function. Indicates adequacy of thyroid hormone production.
Optimal Ranges: ABIM: Low <0.59 ng/dL; Normal 0.59–1.22; Optimal 1.1–1.22; High >1.22 ng/dL. No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: May indicate thyrotoxicosis from hyperthyroidism or thyroiditis. Symptoms: weight loss, rapid/irregular heartbeat, increased bowel movements.
Low Levels Mean: May suggest hypothyroidism from autoimmune diseases, inadequate iodine, or certain medications. Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, cold sensitivity, low heart rate.
Health Implications: Thyroid hormones vital to metabolism, heart/digestive functions, muscle control, brain development, bone maintenance.
How to Improve: Lower high: reduce iodine, increase cruciferous vegetables. Raise low: adequate iodine and vitamin B2; increase protein (valine, arginine, leucine); abstain from alcohol.
References: Cleveland Clinic; MedlinePlus; ABIM; Thyroid Cancer Alliance; Mayo Clinic; NIH/PubMed

Name: Thyroid Peroxidase Antibody (TPOAb)
Description: Antibodies produced when the immune system mistakenly targets the thyroid gland. TPO is an enzyme in the thyroid crucial for T4 and T3 synthesis. Elevated TPOAb indicates potential thyroid dysfunction and possible autoimmune disease.
What It Measures: Antibodies against thyroid peroxidase enzyme — reveals whether the immune system is attacking thyroid tissue and the degree of autoimmune thyroid response.
Optimal Ranges: Optimal: <4 IU/mL; Normal: <9.0 IU/mL; High: >9 IU/mL (Mayo Clinic Laboratories). No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Autoimmune thyroid disease. ~90% with elevated TPOAb have autoimmune disease; ~10% do not have thyroid disease. May indicate Hashimoto's or Graves' disease; rarely cancer.
Low Levels Mean: Low TPOAb (<9.0 IU/mL) suggests thyroid dysfunction is not autoimmune in origin.
Health Implications: Hashimoto's: weight gain, fatigue, hair loss, cold intolerance, irregular periods, constipation, depression. Graves': weight loss, bulging eyes, tremors, heat intolerance, anxiety, increased heart rate.
How to Improve: Ensure adequate folate (B9) intake. Increase carnitine intake. Both show potential for lowering elevated TPOAb.
References: Mayo Clinic Laboratories; MedlinePlus; Cleveland Clinic; British Thyroid Foundation; NCBI/PubMed

Name: Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST)
Description: An enzyme important for amino acid metabolism found in liver, heart, kidneys, brain, and muscles. Unlike ALT (liver-specific), AST's broad distribution means elevated levels can indicate damage in multiple organs.
What It Measures: Tissue damage across various organs, particularly liver and heart. Evaluated alongside ALT for comprehensive liver health assessment.
Optimal Ranges: Adults: ~13–39 U/L. Women typically have lower reference ranges than men. Ranges vary by laboratory.
High Levels Mean: Liver damage (hepatitis, cirrhosis), heart disease/damage, kidney problems, muscle damage, brain injury.
Low Levels Mean: Not specifically addressed.
Health Implications: Monitoring AST helps diagnose hepatitis, cirrhosis, and heart diseases. Aids in evaluating disease severity and treatment effectiveness.
How to Improve: No specific recommendations provided on the page.
References: Cleveland Clinic; Medscape; Kalas MA et al. (2021)

Name: Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN)
Description: Measures the amount of nitrogen (a waste product from protein breakdown) in blood. When the body processes protein it creates urea, which kidneys normally filter out.
What It Measures: Urea accumulation in blood — indicator of kidney filtration function. Can fluctuate due to dehydration, medications, and other factors.
Optimal Ranges: General adult: 7–20 mg/dL; Ages 1–17: 7–20 mg/dL; Adult males: 7–25 mg/dL; Adult females: 6–21 mg/dL.
High Levels Mean: Acute or chronic kidney disease, dehydration, congestive heart failure, urinary tract obstruction, GI bleeding, tissue damage, Addison's disease, high-protein diet.
Low Levels Mean: Liver damage, malnutrition, low-protein diet, overhydration.
Health Implications: Vital for detecting and managing kidney function conditions; helps identify complications early and track disease progression.
How to Improve: Balanced diet (fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins). Maintain adequate hydration. Adjust protein intake. Limit kidney-damaging substances. Manage diabetes, hypertension, heart disease. Regular check-ups.
References: American Kidney Fund; University of Rochester; Mayo Clinic Labs; Cleveland Clinic; National Kidney Foundation; HealthLink BC

Name: Bilirubin
Description: A yellowish substance produced during breakdown of old or damaged red blood cells in the liver. Plays a crucial role in waste removal and serves as indicator of liver function.
What It Measures: Total bilirubin levels including conjugated (water-soluble) and unconjugated (water-insoluble) forms.
Optimal Ranges: Normal serum total: 0.3–1.0 mg/dL; Critically high: >2.5 mg/dL. No tiered or sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Liver dysfunction/disease, bile duct obstruction, jaundice, increased RBC breakdown, hemolytic anemia. ~10% of population has Gilbert's syndrome (harmless elevation). Some research suggests cardioprotective antioxidant effects.
Low Levels Mean: May suggest certain medications, genetic disorders, or conditions affecting RBC production. Low levels generally not a concern.
Health Implications: Used to diagnose and monitor liver diseases, bile duct conditions, RBC disorders. Included in SiPhox Cardiometabolic+ Panel.
How to Improve: Balanced diet with fiber, meat, dairy, healthy fats. Avoid saturated fats, sugars, high-calorie meals. Maintain healthy weight. Exercise regularly. Limit alcohol.
References: MedlinePlus; ABIM; University of Rochester; Cleveland Clinic; Mayo Clinic; NIH/PMC; American Liver Foundation

Name: Progesterone
Description: A hormone produced primarily by ovaries in females, with smaller amounts from adrenal glands. Fluctuates throughout menstrual cycle, peaking during luteal phase. Critical for menstrual cycle, fertility, pregnancy, mood, and sleep.
What It Measures: Progesterone levels in ng/mL — monitors reproductive hormone status and overall hormonal balance.
Optimal Ranges: Female follicular: 0.02–0.9 ng/mL; Female luteal: 2–30 ng/mL; Male adult: 0.12–0.3 ng/mL (ABIM). No tiered breakdown.
High Levels Mean: Elevated during luteal phase or pregnancy (normal). In non-pregnant: may indicate ovarian cysts, adrenal disorders, or certain medications.
Low Levels Mean: May indicate anovulation, luteal phase defect, or hormonal disorders. Medical evaluation essential.
Health Implications: Inadequate progesterone can cause menstrual irregularities, infertility, increased miscarriage risk. Balanced levels crucial for reproductive health.
How to Improve: Foods rich in zinc, vitamin C, B vitamins, magnesium. Higher synthetic folate intake correlates with higher luteal progesterone. Stress management: mindfulness, meditation, deep breathing, adequate sleep.
References: Cleveland Clinic; NCBI/PubMed studies; ABIM; Osmosis from Elsevier

Name: DNA Methylation
Description: A biological process that modifies DNA by adding a methyl group, affecting gene expression without altering DNA sequence. Functions as regulatory mechanism for gene activation/suppression. Critical for development, aging, and gene expression.
What It Measures: Biological age through analysis of methylation patterns at specific DNA sites using the 'epigenetic clock' methodology. Provides estimate of physiological vs chronological age.
Optimal Ranges: No specific numerical ranges. Biological age lower than chronological = healthy lifestyle, lower disease risk. Biological age higher = higher condition risk, greater bodily stress.
High Levels Mean: Body is aging faster than expected, potentially higher risk for age-related diseases.
Low Levels Mean: Healthier aging process and potentially lower risk for age-related diseases.
Health Implications: Provides insights into disease risk, longevity, overall health trajectory. Identifies if body is aging faster due to lifestyle, environment, or genetics, enabling early intervention.
How to Improve: Colorful diet rich in fruits, vegetables, seeds, lean proteins; focus on folate, B12, polyphenols. Regular exercise. Stress management. Avoid environmental toxins. Quality sleep.
References: Aanes H et al. (Sci Rep 2023); Jylhava J et al. (EBioMedicine 2017); Bocklandt S et al. (PLoS ONE 2011); CDC; University of Glasgow

Name: Cadmium
Description: A toxic heavy metal from industrial processes like mining, smelting, battery/plastic production. Not essential for human health; accumulates in the body causing severe damage.
What It Measures: Blood cadmium levels to assess exposure and accumulation of this toxic heavy metal.
Optimal Ranges: Normal: <0.75 ug/L (ZRT Laboratory). No tiered or sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Occupational exposure, smoking (major source), consumption of contaminated food or water.
Low Levels Mean: Not specifically addressed (low cadmium is the goal).
Health Implications: Kidney dysfunction/damage, bone demineralization and osteoporosis, respiratory issues, increased cancer risk (lung, prostate, kidney). Chronic exposure leads to kidney damage, bone demineralization, cancer.
How to Improve: Avoid shellfish, liver, kidney meats. Ensure adequate iron intake (iron deficiency increases cadmium absorption). Protective equipment in high-risk industries. Quit smoking.
References: ATSDR Cadmium Toxicity (2012); ATSDR Case Studies; OSHA Cadmium Guidelines

Name: Copper
Description: An essential mineral vital for functioning of many enzymes and overall health. Crucial for iron metabolism, RBC formation, healthy bones, blood vessels, nerves, immune function, brain development, connective tissue.
What It Measures: Blood serum levels of copper in mg/L.
Optimal Ranges: Normal: 0.64–1.10 mg/L (ZRT Laboratory). No tiered or sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Copper toxicity from excessive intake, supplementation, or genetic conditions like Wilson's disease. Can cause GI distress, liver damage, neurological symptoms.
Low Levels Mean: Copper deficiency from poor intake, malabsorption, or Menkes disease. Can lead to anemia, cardiovascular abnormalities, bone defects, compromised immune function.
Health Implications: Copper imbalances tied to Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, and cancer.
How to Improve: Dietary: beef liver, shellfish, dark chocolate, nuts, seeds, whole grains, potatoes, leafy greens. Supplementation: consult provider; take zinc with copper. If elevated: avoid copper pipes and cookware.
References: ZRT Laboratory; NIH; Harvard School of Public Health; WebMD

Name: Magnesium
Description: An essential mineral and the fourth most abundant in the human body. Functions as a cofactor in over 300 enzymatic reactions.
What It Measures: Blood magnesium levels indicating concentration of this critical mineral in circulation.
Optimal Ranges: In Range: 0–6.98 ug/L; Out of Range: >6.98 ug/L (ZRT Laboratory). No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Relatively uncommon; may result from excessive supplementation or kidney dysfunction.
Low Levels Mean: May stem from poor dietary intake, GI diseases, chronic alcoholism, or certain medications.
Health Implications: Muscle cramps/fatigue, increased cardiovascular disease risk, migraines, neurological disruption, Type 2 Diabetes risk, bone health deterioration.
How to Improve: Leafy greens, nuts, seeds, whole grains, legumes (pumpkin seeds 156 mg/oz, chia seeds 111 mg/oz). Reduce alcohol. Manage stress. Supplements: aspartate, citrate, lactate, chloride absorb better than oxide/sulfate.
References: Al Alawi AM et al. (Int J Endocrinol 2018); Rivlin RS (J Am Coll Nutr 1994); Cuciureanu MD & Vink R (U of Adelaide Press 2011)

Name: Mercury
Description: A toxic heavy metal found in elemental, inorganic, and organic forms. Highly toxic, particularly to the nervous system.
What It Measures: Mercury levels in the body to assess exposure risk.
Optimal Ranges: Normal: <6.98 ug/L (ZRT Laboratory). No tiered or sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Elevated from dietary sources (fish/shellfish), occupational exposure, or certain traditional medicines.
Low Levels Mean: Not specifically addressed (low mercury is the goal).
Health Implications: Neurological and behavioral disorders, severe kidney damage, impaired fetal and child development, bioaccumulation in tissues over time.
How to Improve: Avoid mercury-containing products. Limit high-mercury fish (shark, swordfish, king mackerel, tilefish); choose salmon, cod, sardines, trout. Follow occupational safety guidelines.
References: EPA Mercury (2024)

Name: Selenium
Description: An essential trace mineral important for antioxidant enzyme production protecting against oxidative stress and inflammation. Plays a role in thyroid hormone metabolism and immune function.
What It Measures: Serum selenium levels in ug/L.
Optimal Ranges: Normal: 130–362 ug/L (ZRT Laboratory). No tiered or sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Excessive intake (often supplements) can lead to toxicity: GI distress, hair loss, neurological damage.
Low Levels Mean: Deficiency from poor intake, certain diseases, GI conditions. Associated with increased heart disease risk, thyroid dysfunction, weakened immune response.
Health Implications: Supports immune function, thyroid health, reproductive health. Hypothesized role against cancer, cardiovascular disease, thyroid disease, cognitive decline.
How to Improve: Brazil nuts (1 oz = 544 mcg), seafood, meat, eggs, grains. Supplementation if dietary intake insufficient. Limit alcohol.
References: NIH Selenium (2021)

Name: Zinc
Description: An essential trace element involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions. Plays critical roles in immune function, taste/smell perception, growth and development across all life stages.
What It Measures: Blood zinc levels for metabolic processes including protein synthesis, DNA synthesis, and cell division.
Optimal Ranges: Normal: 5.06–8.57 mg/L (ZRT Laboratory). No tiered or sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Toxicity: nausea, vomiting, appetite loss, stomach cramps, diarrhea, headaches. Chronic high intake interferes with copper absorption, potentially causing copper deficiency, anemia, weakened immunity.
Low Levels Mean: Impaired immune function, increased infection susceptibility, growth retardation in children, delayed sexual maturation, hair loss, diarrhea, eye/skin lesions, appetite loss, impaired wound healing.
Health Implications: Maintains structural protein integrity, protects cells from oxidative damage, supports wound healing and immune defense.
How to Improve: Meat, shellfish, dairy, nuts, seeds, legumes, whole grains; oysters contain more than daily recommended intake per serving. Supplementation if needed (consult provider re: interactions).
References: Chen B et al. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy (2024)

Name: Iron Saturation
Description: Measures the percentage of transferrin (main iron transport protein) currently carrying iron. Reflects how effectively the body distributes available iron. Calculated using serum iron and TIBC.
What It Measures: Percentage of transferrin bound to iron, iron distribution efficiency, iron availability and transport.
Optimal Ranges: Standard Lab: Low <20%; In range 20–50%; High >50%. SiPhox: Low <15%; In range 15–55%; High >55%. No sex-specific ranges.
High Levels Mean: Iron overload; over time can contribute to hemochromatosis, liver injury, joint pain, heart dysfunction.
Low Levels Mean: Iron deficiency or anemia. Symptoms: tiredness, lightheadedness, shortness of breath, poor concentration.
Health Implications: Critical for oxygen delivery (hemoglobin), cellular energy production, immune function. More balanced view than serum iron alone (which fluctuates daily).
How to Improve: Low: iron-rich foods (red meat, poultry, lentils, beans, tofu, leafy greens), pair with vitamin C, cast iron cookware, avoid tea/coffee/calcium with meals, iron supplements. High: limit red meat, avoid iron supplements, reduce vitamin C supplementation.
References: NIH; Mayo Clinic; Medscape

Name: ApoB:ApoA1 Ratio
Description: Compares ApoB to ApoA1 levels. Considered stronger than total cholesterol/HDL and LDL/HDL ratios for predicting cardiovascular risk. Highly valuable for detecting atherogenic risk.
What It Measures: Ratio of ApoB to ApoA1 in blood to assess cardiovascular disease risk. Lower ratios = lower disease risk.
Optimal Ranges: Male: Low risk <0.77; Moderate 0.77–0.95; High risk >0.95. Female: Low risk <0.63; Moderate 0.63–0.78; High risk >0.78. General consensus: Optimal <0.8.
High Levels Mean: Increased risk for metabolic syndrome, coronary artery disease, coronary heart disease, all-cause mortality.
Low Levels Mean: Reduced cardiovascular risk and healthier apolipoprotein profiles.
Health Implications: Elevated ratios associated with multiple cardiovascular and metabolic conditions. Valuable screening marker.
How to Improve: Mediterranean or vegan diet; fermented dairy; limit sugar; higher protein; polyunsaturated fats; adequate vitamin D, C, magnesium; regular exercise; improved sleep hygiene.
References: Mayo Clinic Labs; PubMed; Academic research on metabolic syndrome/coronary disease/mortality

Name: LDL:ApoB Ratio
Description: Reveals LDL particle size and density by dividing LDL cholesterol by ApoB (both in mg/dL). Since each LDL particle contains one ApoB molecule, ApoB directly counts particle number while LDL measures total cholesterol amount.
What It Measures: LDL particle size and density distribution — whether cholesterol is across many small particles or fewer large ones.
Optimal Ranges: >1.3 = Large buoyant LDL (Pattern A) — lower CV risk; 1.2–1.3 = Mixed — intermediate risk; <1.2 = Small dense LDL (Pattern B) — higher CV risk. Note: Even with favorable ratios ApoB >90 mg/dL indicates increased risk.
High Levels Mean: Fewer, larger LDL particles carrying cholesterol — lower cardiovascular risk.
Low Levels Mean: More numerous, smaller, denser LDL particles — greater CV risk. These penetrate arterial walls more easily, remain in circulation longer, oxidize more readily, trigger inflammatory responses.
Health Implications: People with lower ratios face significantly higher coronary artery disease risks even with normal LDL levels — explains why some with acceptable numbers still experience heart attacks.
How to Improve: Reduce refined carbs and sugar; replace with healthy fats. Combine aerobic + resistance training (150+ min/week). 5–10% weight reduction improves particle distribution. Medications: statins, fibrates/niacin, PCSK9 inhibitors.
References: PubMed Central; Multiple studies on small dense LDL as cardiovascular biomarker
